other_observers: Ada Jones.  After obtaining confirmatory evidence we contacted another local birder (Dave Price) and got him on the bird.  He put the word out to local birders such that many others were able to observe the bird.  We stayed on locale until later birders arrived so we could get them on the bird.
description: Found and identified by Linda Chittum and Ada Jones.  First observed perched in a snag at approximately 10 m distance when we were driving down a country road.  We stopped and got binoculars on the bird.  Obvious kingbird and from slightly above and viewing the bird from the back the dark blackish cap immediately stood out.  The lack of any white terminal tail band immediately removed any consideration of EAKI as did apparent bulk and size.  The bird turned it's head and body and immediately the huge, thick black bill and yellow coloration ventrally grabbed my attention whereupon I immediately exited the vehicle and proceeded slowly down the road to improve my angle and started taking photos.  The bird moved to a different tree nearby and shortly thereafter we heard it vocalizing.  For the next approximately 3 hours we watched the bird as it moved around the area, perching in different trees and periodically calling.  I'll spare a description of the call and attach audio file.  After calling Dave Price, a local birder living fairly close, we kept on the bird until he arrived.  However, it just disappeared for about 45 minutes until I eventually relocated it further west on the same road after hearing it's call.  Additional local birders were notified and Linda and Ada stayed around watching the bird until other birders arrived and getting them on the bird. Identification features include very large, thick black bill.  Blackish cap, darkest around the eye, transitioning to more olive-brown at rear of crown and solid olive-brown coloration dorsally.  Flight feathers more brownish color with very thin pale margin on tertials.  The tail was of similar coloration.  White throat transitioning to light gray and yellow upper breast.  From there smooth, fairly bright yellow through undertail coverts.  The underwing coverts are yellowish.  After reviewing photos this is clearly an adult bird, despite the brighter yellow coloration ventrally.  The outer primaries (P8 - P10) are emarginated though I lack experience and knowledge in judging whether this degree might be sufficient to conclude a male.  Of interest is the ongoing secondary molt with some slightly brown/rusty secondaries, feathers still in sheath, and partial growth of new secondaries.
